refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-006 (Refined): The NGHE solution shall generate and maintain a real-time worksite model representing terrain elevation, static obstacles (rocks, structures, vegetation), dynamic hazards (personnel, other equipment), and work-zone boundaries, with sufficient fidelity to support autonomous path planning and operator situational awareness.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on fidelity requirements, update frequency, and intended consumers. Refinement clarifies that the model must serve both planning (offline) and execution (real-time) functions, must distinguish static from dynamic features, and must be safety-critical (high criticality factor). This enables verification against acceptance criteria: 'worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features.'
refined_fr.verification_idea: Acceptance test: (1) Model generated within 5 min of site survey; (2) Terrain elevation accuracy ±0.5 m; (3) Obstacle detection coverage ≥95% for objects >0.3 m; (4) Model accessible via Telemetry interface to planning and execution subsystems; (5) Safety-critical features (hazard zones) flagged and logged.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire worksite sensor data (LiDAR, camera, GNSS) and fuse into unified coordinate frame.
CR-NGHE-006.2: Classify and segment terrain, obstacles, and work zones from fused sensor data.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Maintain and update worksite model in real-time as new sensor data arrives.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Expose worksite model via standardized interface (Telemetry) to planning and execution consumers.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Flag and alert Safety Services when hazardous features (personnel proximity, unstable terrain) are detected in the model.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized mapping service (on-vehicle compute) that fuses multi-sensor data (LiDAR, camera, GNSS) into a 3D occupancy grid and publishes via Telemetry interface.
DP-B: Distributed edge mapping (vehicle + tele-operator station) where vehicle collects raw sensor data, tele-operator performs fusion and classification, and publishes model back to vehicle.
DP-C: Cloud-based mapping service where vehicle streams raw sensor data to cloud, cloud performs fusion/classification, and pushes model back to vehicle and planning systems via Telemetry.
DP-D: Hybrid mapping (vehicle + Safety Services) where vehicle performs local sensor fusion, Safety Services performs hazard classification and alerts, and model is shared via Telemetry.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized on-vehicle mapping service with multi-sensor fusion (LiDAR, camera, GNSS) into 3D occupancy grid, published via Telemetry interface to planning/execution and Safety Services.

potential_sources: Sensor data acquisition (LiDAR, camera, GNSS) is decoupled from fusion logic via standardized sensor driver interfaces.
Fusion algorithm is decoupled from model representation (occupancy grid is a standard format).
Model publication is decoupled from consumption via Telemetry interface (planning, execution, Safety Services are independent consumers).
Real-time update loop is self-contained on vehicle, reducing latency and network dependency.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A avoids cloud latency (DP-C) and tele-operator bottleneck (DP-B), critical for safety-critical real-time hazard detection. Hybrid (DP-D) introduces unnecessary coupling between vehicle mapping and Safety Services; hazard classification should be a separate concern.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle has sufficient on-board compute (GPU/FPGA) to perform real-time multi-sensor fusion at ≥10 Hz.
Assumption: LiDAR, camera, and GNSS are available on vehicle and calibrated to common coordinate frame.
Risk: If on-board compute is insufficient, fusion latency may exceed real-time requirements, degrading hazard detection.
Risk: Sensor calibration drift over time may degrade model accuracy; periodic recalibration procedure required.
Risk: Occupancy grid resolution vs. memory trade-off; coarse grid may miss small hazards, fine grid may exceed compute/memory budget.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Specify on-board compute platform (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin or equivalent) and occupancy grid resolution (e.g., 0.1 m voxels) in DP to enable feasibility assessment. Add requirement for sensor calibration verification at startup and periodic drift detection.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: On-vehicle mapping service module: integrates LiDAR, camera, GNSS sensor drivers; performs real-time multi-sensor fusion into 3D occupancy grid; maintains and updates model state; publishes model via Telemetry interface to external consumers (planning, execution, Safety Services). Includes sensor data acquisition, fusion algorithm, model storage, and Telemetry publisher.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle publishes worksite model (3D occupancy grid, terrain elevation, obstacle locations, work-zone boundaries, hazard flags) to planning/execution functions and Safety Services. Frequency: ≥10 Hz for real-time updates.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives mapping task parameters (survey area bounds, sensor configuration, update frequency) from planning/execution. Used to configure mapping service at mission start.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle publishes safety-critical hazard detections (personnel proximity, unstable terrain, work-zone violations) to Safety Services for real-time alerting and intervention.

module_containment_scores.notes: Mapping service is core vehicle capability; sensor fusion, model maintenance, and real-time updates are tightly coupled to vehicle state and sensor suite. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry/SafetyAlert publication crosses boundary. High containment justified by safety criticality and real-time latency requirements.

Safety Services should consume model (via Telemetry) and perform hazard classification/alerting, not generate the model. Assigning mapping to Safety Services would couple sensor fusion to safety logic, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion risk: Safety Services would need direct access to vehicle sensors and compute, creating architectural coupling.

Teleoperation is for remote operator control, not mapping. Assigning mapping to Teleoperation would introduce unnecessary latency (operator station may be remote) and couple operator interface to sensor fusion. High diffusion: tele-operator station may lack compute resources for real-time fusion.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Mapping could be assigned here as a system-level service, but this would diffuse the implementation across multiple physical/software boundaries (vehicle sensors, on-board compute, network publication). Better to assign to Vehicle (the physical platform where sensors and compute reside) and expose via Telemetry interface.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Worksite mapping service is best contained within Vehicle module because: (1) Sensors (LiDAR, camera, GNSS) are vehicle-mounted; (2) Real-time fusion requires low-latency access to sensor data; (3) Model is a vehicle-centric representation of the environment; (4) Safety-critical hazard detection depends on model freshness, which is maximized by on-vehicle processing. External consumers (planning, execution, Safety Services) access model via Telemetry interface, maintaining loose coupling.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle module may become overloaded if mapping service consumes significant compute/memory, leaving insufficient resources for vehicle control and navigation. Mitigation: Specify compute budget (e.g., max 30% CPU, 2 GB RAM) and monitor at runtime.

Risk: Sensor calibration and maintenance are vehicle-specific; if mapping service is tightly coupled to vehicle hardware, changes to sensor suite may require significant rework. Mitigation: Use abstraction layer (sensor driver interface) to decouple fusion algorithm from specific sensor models.

Risk: Model publication via Telemetry may create network bottleneck if model size is large (e.g., high-resolution occupancy grid). Mitigation: Implement model compression/streaming and adaptive resolution based on network bandwidth.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify Vehicle module scope to include 'on-vehicle mapping service' as a distinct sub-component with defined interfaces (sensor inputs, Telemetry/SafetyAlert outputs). Specify compute platform and resource budget. Define sensor driver abstraction layer to enable sensor suite flexibility.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (centralized on-vehicle mapping with multi-sensor fusion) directly addresses refined FR (generate and maintain real-time worksite model). DP specifies how (sensor fusion, occupancy grid, Telemetry publication) and where (on-vehicle). Coupling check passes: sensor acquisition, fusion, and publication are logically decoupled. Assumptions and risks are identified and manageable (compute platform, sensor calibration, grid resolution).

diffusion_risks: Telemetry interface publication may create network bottleneck if model size exceeds available bandwidth. Mitigation: Implement adaptive compression and streaming.

SafetyAlert publication couples Vehicle mapping to Safety Services; if Safety Services is unavailable, hazard alerts may not reach operators. Mitigation: Implement local hazard logging and fallback alerting on vehicle.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module is the correct containment boundary. Mapping service is tightly coupled to vehicle sensors and compute, and must operate in real-time with low latency. Assigning to Safety Services, Teleoperation, or NGHE Solution would introduce unacceptable diffusion and latency. Telemetry/SafetyAlert interfaces are appropriate for loose coupling to external consumers.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via acceptance tests: (1) Model generation time, (2) Terrain accuracy, (3) Obstacle detection coverage, (4) Interface availability (Telemetry, SafetyAlert), (5) Real-time update frequency. All tests can be performed on vehicle with sensor data playback or live survey.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle module owns mapping service; planning/execution and Safety Services are consumers via Telemetry/SafetyAlert. Ownership boundaries are well-defined. Interface contracts (Telemetry format, SafetyAlert schema, Tasking parameters) must be specified in detailed design.

recommended_revisions: Specify Telemetry interface schema (occupancy grid format, coordinate frame, update frequency, compression method).
Specify SafetyAlert schema (hazard type, location, severity, timestamp).
Specify Tasking interface schema (survey area bounds, sensor configuration, update frequency).
Define compute platform and resource budget for on-vehicle mapping service.
Define sensor calibration procedure and drift detection mechanism.
Define occupancy grid resolution and memory budget.

decision.justification: Refined FR is not atomic; it contains five sub-FRs (sensor data acquisition, classification, model maintenance, interface publication, hazard alerting). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to DP and IM. DP-A is sound and well-coupled, and Vehicle is the correct IM boundary. Proceed to next decomposition level for sub-FRs CR-NGHE-006.1 through CR-NGHE-006.5.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire worksite sensor data (LiDAR, camera, GNSS) and fuse into unified coordinate frame.
CR-NGHE-006.2: Classify and segment terrain, obstacles, and work zones from fused sensor data.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Maintain and update worksite model in real-time as new sensor data arrives.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Expose worksite model via standardized interface (Telemetry) to planning and execution consumers.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Flag and alert Safety Services when hazardous features (personnel proximity, unstable terrain) are detected in the model.

assumptions: Vehicle has sufficient on-board compute (GPU/FPGA) to perform real-time multi-sensor fusion at ≥10 Hz.
LiDAR, camera, and GNSS are available on vehicle and calibrated to common coordinate frame.
Telemetry interface is available and has sufficient bandwidth for model publication.
Safety Services is available to consume SafetyAlert messages; fallback alerting is implemented on vehicle.
Occupancy grid is an acceptable model representation for planning/execution and Safety Services.
open_risks: On-board compute may be insufficient for real-time fusion; feasibility assessment required.
Sensor calibration drift may degrade model accuracy over time; periodic recalibration procedure required.
Occupancy grid resolution vs. memory trade-off; optimal resolution must be determined.
Network bandwidth may be insufficient for high-resolution model publication; compression/streaming required.
Safety Services availability and response time for hazard alerts; fallback mechanism required.
evidence_needed: Compute platform specification and performance benchmarks for multi-sensor fusion.
Sensor calibration procedure and drift detection mechanism.
Occupancy grid resolution analysis (accuracy vs. memory/compute trade-off).
Network bandwidth analysis and model compression strategy.
Safety Services interface specification and response time requirements.
Acceptance test results for model generation time, accuracy, and interface availability.
